suspects.alibi: I had a client call from 2:30 to 3:30, then I was reviewing ad copy at my desk. I'm in the advertising wing — I rarely go to the reporters' floor.

starter: The reporters' bullpen has a badge reader on its only door. The access log shows three entries between 2 PM and 4 PM: Lisa's badge at 2:03 PM, Marcus's badge at 2:47 PM, and Howard's badge at 3:31 PM. Priya's desk and the USB drive are inside the bullpen.

The newsroom's print server auto-timestamps every job with the workstation ID. A document titled 'Narayan_Investigation_Draft.docx' — a file from Priya's drive — was printed at 2:18 PM from a workstation inside the bullpen. Priya was confirmed at a press briefing from 2:00 to 3:45 PM and did not initiate the job.

The copy machine logs usage by employee ID. Marcus's ID shows continuous jobs from 2:07 to 2:43 PM — the machine is one hallway outside the bullpen. Howard was in the editorial meeting until 3:29 PM, confirmed by four attendees who signed the meeting sheet.

exonerations.howard: Howard's badge shows him entering the editorial meeting room at 1:58 PM. Four attendees confirm he was present until 3:29 PM. His bullpen entry at 3:31 was well after the 2:18 PM print job.
exonerations.glen-n: The photo editing suite has its own badge log; Glen's card shows continuous presence from 1:27 PM to 4:02 PM without leaving. He never badged into the reporters' bullpen.
exonerations.marcus-n: The copy machine log places Marcus running jobs from 2:07 to 2:43 PM — squarely during the 2:18 print job. His bullpen badge entry at 2:47 came after the fact.
exonerations.sheila: Sheila's desk is in the advertising wing, which has its own badge perimeter. Her card shows no entries to the reporters' bullpen at any point Thursday.

reveal: No clue named Lisa — you had to triangulate. Clue 1 shows only three people entered the bullpen between 2 and 4 PM. Clue 2 places the act at 2:18 PM — Priya's investigation file printed from inside the bullpen while Priya was at the briefing. Clue 3 eliminates two of the three bullpen entrants from the 2:18 window: Marcus was at the copy machine from 2:07 to 2:43, and Howard was in the editorial meeting until 3:29. That leaves only Lisa, whose badge shows she entered at 2:03 — 15 minutes before the print job fired. She admitted walking past Priya's desk around 3, but her own badge log shows she was in the bullpen long before any coffee run.

title: The Locker Room Lift
case: A Rolex watch valued at $6,500 was stolen from member Warren Holt's locker at Pinehurst Country Club between 9 AM (when Warren left for the course) and 12:30 PM (when he returned to find it missing). The locker was unlocked but closed.

suspects.name: Tad
suspects.alibi: I was on the driving range giving a lesson from 9 to 10:30. Then I grabbed lunch in the clubhouse.

suspects.name: Patty
suspects.alibi: I was restocking towels and cleaning from 8 to 10 AM. Everything was fine when I left.

suspects.name: Rodrigo
suspects.alibi: I fixed a leaky showerhead in the locker room around 11. I was in and out of that area for maybe 20 minutes.

suspects.name: Harvey
suspects.alibi: I teed off with Warren, played 9 holes, then came back inside at 11 for a drink. Warren kept going.

suspects.name: Neil
suspects.alibi: I finished my round around 10:30 and spent some time in the locker room before heading to the grill.

starter: The men's locker room uses keypad fobs for access. The log shows four entries between 9 AM and 12:30 PM: Patty's staff fob at 8:02 AM and exit at 10:04 AM; Neil's member fob at 10:33 AM and exit at 10:46 AM; a maintenance staff fob at 10:52 AM; and Harvey's member fob at 11:03 AM and exit at 11:22 AM.

A hallway camera outside the locker room entrance shows the 10:52 AM maintenance entrant carrying a toolbag into the locker room. The same individual exits at 10:58 AM — then re-enters at 11:04 AM without the toolbag, exiting again at 11:16 AM.

The club's maintenance dispatch log shows one locker room work order for the day: 'leaky showerhead, Stall 3,' submitted at 10:48 AM and assigned to the on-call technician. The job close-out was filed at 11:00 AM. The second entry at 11:04 AM has no corresponding work order.

exonerations.tad: Tad's golf lesson is in the club's scheduling system from 9:00 to 10:30 AM, confirmed by the student. The clubhouse dining camera places him at a table from 10:45 AM to approximately 1:00 PM.
exonerations.patty: Patty's keycard shows her exit from the locker room at 10:04 AM. The staff break room log confirms she was on break from 10:10 AM and didn't return to the locker room before her shift ended at 2 PM.
exonerations.harvey: Harvey was on the course with Warren from 9 AM — confirmed by the starter's tee-time log — and his member fob shows his first locker room entry at 11:03 AM, after both maintenance visits.
exonerations.neil-k: Neil's member fob shows he entered at 10:33 AM and exited at 10:46 AM — six minutes before the 10:52 AM maintenance fob entry. The grill room camera places him at the bar from 10:51 AM onward.

reveal: No clue named Rodrigo — you had to triangulate. Clue 1 shows a maintenance staff fob entering at 10:52 AM, timed exactly to Rodrigo's dispatch. Clue 3 shows the work order was officially closed at 11:00 AM — the assigned job was done. But Clue 2 shows the same person re-entered at 11:04 AM without his toolbag and didn't leave until 11:16 — a second visit with no open work order and no tools. Warren's locker is in the area serviced near Stall 3. Neil left by 10:46, Patty by 10:04, Harvey arrived at 11:03 — after both maintenance visits. Rodrigo's second, tool-free entry after his job was officially closed is when the watch was taken.

title: The Clinic Shortage
case: A vial of ketamine — a controlled surgical anesthetic — went missing from the locked drug cabinet at Millbrook Animal Clinic. Director Dr. Park discovered the shortage during the mandatory 5 PM controlled-substance count. The cabinet had been accessed legitimately during two afternoon procedures.

suspects.name: Dr. Park
suspects.alibi: I performed the morning surgeries and logged everything by the book. I was in consultation rooms all afternoon seeing non-surgical appointments.

suspects.name: Dr. Reyes
suspects.alibi: I did the afternoon procedures — a cat spay at 1 PM and a dog orthopedic at 3. Both went smoothly. I was in the recovery room monitoring the ortho patient after.

suspects.name: Keisha
suspects.alibi: I pulled medications for Dr. Park's morning cases and logged everything correctly. My shift ended at 2.

suspects.name: Nolan
suspects.alibi: I assisted Dr. Reyes with both afternoon procedures. I handle the drug draw-up and documentation for afternoon cases.

suspects.name: Ingrid
suspects.alibi: I'm at the front desk — I don't have a code to the drug cabinet. That's clinical staff only.

starter: The controlled-substance cabinet requires dual signatures — the veterinarian's and the vet tech's — for every withdrawal. The afternoon log shows two completed dual-entry records signed by Dr. Reyes and Nolan for the 1 PM and 3 PM procedures. A third ketamine entry appears at 4:44 PM, signed only by Nolan, with the procedure field blank.

The clinic's afternoon surgery schedule lists exactly two procedures: the 1 PM cat spay (closed at 1:48 PM) and the 3 PM orthopedic (closed at 4:20 PM). No third procedure was scheduled or performed. The 4:44 PM cabinet entry occurred 24 minutes after the last procedure closed.

The drug cabinet's keypad requires two codes entered simultaneously — the veterinarian's and the vet tech's. The 4:44 PM entry was made with only one code. The dual-code alert, which normally fires on single-code attempts, was suppressed at 4:41 PM from the clinic's admin terminal. Nolan's workstation is the assigned admin terminal. At 4:44 PM, Dr. Reyes was in the recovery room completing post-op monitoring notes — with timed entries at 4:40 and 4:50 PM.

exonerations.dr-park: Dr. Park's afternoon is logged in the appointment system: six patient check-ins under her name between 2 PM and 4:30 PM, each confirmed by the intake log. She was in consultation rooms continuously and never approached the drug cabinet after the morning count.
exonerations.dr-reyes: Dr. Reyes co-signed both afternoon procedure records. At 4:44 PM her post-op monitoring sheet shows timed entries at 4:40 and 4:50 — she was in the recovery room. Her code does not appear in the 4:44 cabinet log.
exonerations.keisha: Keisha's shift ended at 2:00 PM. The clinic's punch system confirms she clocked out at 2:03 PM and was off-site before the unauthorized 4:44 entry.
exonerations.ingrid: Ingrid has no cabinet keypad code — clinical staff only. The access log has no entry under her name at any point.

reveal: No clue named Nolan — you had to triangulate. Clue 2 established that no third procedure occurred Thursday afternoon, so there was no legitimate reason to open the cabinet at 4:44 PM. Clue 1 identifies an unauthorized single-signature withdrawal at 4:44 under Nolan's name alone, with no procedure to justify it. Clue 3 shows the dual-code safeguard was disabled at 4:41 PM from Nolan's own admin terminal — three minutes before the withdrawal — and that Dr. Reyes was in the recovery room at 4:44 with documented timed entries. Dr. Park was in consultations; Keisha had clocked out at 2; Ingrid has no code. Nolan disabled the safeguard from his own workstation, then used his code alone to take the vial.

title: The Backstage Grab
case: A vintage guitar strap valued at $1,800 — a tour keepsake belonging to headliner band Hollow Root — disappeared from their backstage dressing room at Meridian Amphitheater between soundcheck (3 PM) and the opening act (7 PM). Tour manager Lena discovered it missing at 7:15 PM.

suspects.name: Lena
suspects.alibi: I was coordinating crew all afternoon. I'm the one who noticed it gone and reported it.

suspects.name: Cy
suspects.alibi: I was in the production office and on the stage floor all afternoon overseeing setup. Busy day before a show.

suspects.name: Dani
suspects.alibi: I was backstage from about 5 PM warming up. I had no reason to go into Hollow Root's dressing room.

suspects.name: Marcus
suspects.alibi: I was posted at the backstage door from 4 PM. Anyone going backstage had to pass me.

suspects.name: Priya
suspects.alibi: I had a 4:30 interview with the band. I was in the dressing room from about 4:28 to 4:55 with the whole band present.

starter: The backstage dressing room is accessible from two points: the main backstage door (logged by Marcus) and a production corridor connecting directly from the production office — used by venue staff for equipment routing and not visible from the backstage door. The corridor has no badge reader.

A band member's Instagram story, timestamped 5:48 PM, shows the guitar strap hanging in the dressing room. A motion sensor in the production corridor — installed for equipment security — logged movement at 6:02 PM and again at 6:09 PM.

Cy's required production sign-off sheet shows his signature at the lighting rig (on the stage) at 5:55 PM and at the PA mixing board (main floor) at 6:15 PM. Between those two sign-offs, Cy has no logged position. The production corridor connects his production office base to the dressing room area and exits naturally near the mixing board.

exonerations.lena-t: A catering dispute required Lena's attention from 5:45 to 6:20 PM — documented in the venue's event management app with three timestamped messages and confirmed by the catering supervisor.
exonerations.dani: Marcus's backstage door log shows Dani entering at 5:03 PM. He confirms she went directly to the opening act's separate dressing room and didn't emerge until 6:45 PM, confirmed by her bassist and the hallway camera.
exonerations.marcus-v: Marcus was at the backstage door continuously from 4 PM, confirmed by radio check-ins with venue security every 15 minutes. He cannot be simultaneously at the door and in the production corridor.
exonerations.priya-b: Marcus's door log shows Priya's press pass entry at 4:28 PM and exit at 4:57 PM. She left 51 minutes before the strap was last photographed at 5:48 PM.

reveal: No clue named Cy — you had to triangulate. Clue 1 established the production corridor as a second, unmonitored route to the dressing room accessible only to venue staff, connecting directly from Cy's production office. Clue 2 placed movement in that corridor at 6:02 and 6:09 PM — after the strap was confirmed present at 5:48. Clue 3 shows Cy's only unlogged window runs from 5:55 (lighting rig) to 6:15 (PA board) — 20 minutes that bracket both sensor hits exactly, and the corridor's exit puts him naturally at the mixing board for the 6:15 sign-off. Lena was resolving a catering dispute, Dani was in her dressing room, Marcus was at the door, Priya had left before the strap was last seen. Only Cy had the access, the route, and the window.

title: The Keynote Caper
case: A prototype USB drive containing unreleased AI software was stolen from keynote speaker Vivian Cole’s hotel room (712) during the Pacific Tech Conference. It vanished between 11 AM and 1 PM while Vivian was on a panel downstairs.

suspects.name: Rosa
suspects.alibi: I cleaned room 712 at 11:15 and it was fine — I saw the drive on the desk. I had six more rooms to do after that and was in 701 through 709 all morning.

suspects.name: Theo
suspects.alibi: I delivered lunch to room 716 at 12:20. I knocked on 712 by mistake first — force of habit — but no one answered and I moved on.

suspects.name: Ingrid
suspects.alibi: I was managing the conference all day — registration desk, AV room, ballroom. I coordinated Vivian’s panel personally. I never left the conference center.

suspects.name: Fletcher
suspects.alibi: I was at the concierge desk from 10 AM to 3 PM. Several guests asked for restaurant recommendations and I arranged two cab pickups. You can check the desk log.

suspects.name: Sasha
suspects.alibi: I went back to my room to rest between sessions. I was tired from the morning panel and took a short nap before the afternoon breakout. I didn’t leave my room.

starter: The hotel keycard system logs every access. Room 712 was accessed three times that day: by Rosa’s housekeeper card at 11:15 AM, and by a guest-tier card at 11:52 AM. Guest-tier floor-7 access cards were issued exclusively to guests in rooms 710–718. Vivian’s own card shows no use until her return at 1:05 PM.

The missing USB drive had a Tile tracker registered to Vivian for insurance purposes. Her Tile app shows a signal ping at 12:10 PM in the lobby elevator bank — a location consistent with someone leaving floor 7 via the main elevator.

The conference schedule included a mandatory 11:30 AM tech-check for all co-presenters in the first-floor AV room. Ingrid’s sign-in sheet shows exactly one co-presenter did not attend. Hotel records show that a floor-7 room was checked out 90 minutes earlier than its stated checkout time that same morning.

exonerations.rosa: Rosa’s housekeeper log shows timestamped entries in rooms 701, 703, 705, and 709 between 11:30 AM and 12:45 PM. The keycard system distinguishes staff cards from guest cards — the 11:52 access was made by a guest-tier card, not a staff card.
exonerations.theo: Theo’s room service order was tied to room 716. The occupant confirmed the delivery and a 9-minute interaction. Theo’s service elevator log shows him in transit between floors at 11:52 AM — he was never on the floor-7 corridor.
exonerations.ingrid: Ingrid’s coordinator access badge logged continuous activity in the conference center: registration desk at 11:35, AV room at 11:50, ballroom at 12:05. She never accessed the hotel elevator system and has no floor-7 room card.
exonerations.fletcher: The concierge desk camera shows Fletcher at his station continuously from 11:30 AM to 12:30 PM. He processed two check-ins and one luggage request during that window and has no guest-tier floor access above the lobby.

reveal: Clue 1 places a guest-tier floor-7 card — not Rosa’s staff card, not Vivian’s card — at room 712 at 11:52 AM. Only guests in rooms 710–718 carry floor-7 guest cards; Sasha was in room 714. Clue 2 shows the Tile moving through the lobby elevator at 12:10 PM, consistent with a floor-7 guest heading down with the drive. Clue 3 confirms exactly one co-presenter skipped the mandatory 11:30 tech check and checked out of their hotel room early that morning — the same person whose alibi (‘resting in my room’) is directly contradicted by the checkout record. Rosa’s housekeeper log, Theo’s elevator records, Ingrid’s badge trail, and Fletcher’s desk camera all place everyone else elsewhere. Sasha had the floor access, the motive (a competing startup), and a departure that erases her alibi entirely.

title: The Genome Grab
case: A backup hard drive containing six months of unpublished genomics research disappeared from Lab 4B at Carver University. The drive was on Dr. Chen’s workstation when she left at 3 PM; by 5 PM, it was gone.

suspects.name: Priya
suspects.alibi: I left around 3:10 and went straight to the library to work on my thesis chapter. I was there all afternoon.

suspects.name: Marcus
suspects.alibi: I had a water heater replacement in Building C all afternoon. I wasn’t near the science building.

suspects.name: Dr. Osei
suspects.alibi: I was in my lab most of the afternoon reviewing grant materials. I stepped into Lab 4B briefly around 3:30 to borrow a pipette stand — I returned it the same day.

suspects.name: Jamie
suspects.alibi: I finished my shift in my own lab at 2:30 and went home. I’ve only been here two weeks and have never been to the east wing.

suspects.name: Nadia
suspects.alibi: I did a standard compliance walkthrough of Lab 4B from 2:45 to 3:05 PM. Then I returned to the admin building to write up my notes.

starter: Carver Science Building requires a key-fob scan to enter each lab. Lab 4B’s access log shows: Priya (8:15 AM to 3:10 PM), Jamie (9:00 AM to 2:30 PM), Nadia (2:45 PM to 3:05 PM), and one entry at 3:22 PM with exit at 3:41 PM. The fob system assigns permissions by role: faculty fobs have cross-wing access, while student fobs are restricted to a single wing.

The lab’s shared printer output one document during the 3:22–3:41 PM window: an equipment sign-out form, printed at 3:28 PM, found abandoned face-down in the tray. Dr. Chen had never used this form — it comes from a different lab’s protocol binder and requires the borrower to identify themselves by name and department.

Dr. Chen recently submitted a grant renewal citing specific sample sizes and sequencing methods that exactly match the stolen drive’s contents. Two days before the theft, a competing grant proposal was publicly pre-registered through the same funding agency by a researcher in the same building. That proposal cites ‘a forthcoming dataset’ with sample parameters identical to Dr. Chen’s unpublished data.

exonerations.priya: Priya’s fob shows exit at 3:10 PM and badge-in at Caldwell Library at 3:15 PM. The library’s entry camera and her study-room reservation confirm she was there from 3:15 PM until 5:30 PM.
exonerations.marcus: Marcus’s work order placed him at Building C for a water heater replacement from 3:00 PM to 5:15 PM. His fob log shows no Science Building access that afternoon, and the Building C supervisor confirmed his presence.
exonerations.jamie: Jamie’s student fob is restricted to the west wing. Lab 4B is in the east wing — the system would have rejected any cross-wing entry attempt. Jamie also had no knowledge of Dr. Chen’s research.
exonerations.nadia: Nadia’s fob confirms she exited Lab 4B at 3:05 PM, seventeen minutes before the 3:22 entry. The grants office visitor log shows her returning to the admin building at 3:12 PM.

reveal: Clue 1 narrows the theft to a single access event at 3:22 PM, made by a fob with cross-wing faculty privileges — only faculty fobs clear the east-wing reader. Clue 2 shows that whoever entered at 3:22 PM printed a sign-out form from a different lab’s protocol binder — bringing their own paperwork suggests they came prepared to create a paper trail for a ‘legitimate’ visit. Clue 3 connects the theft to direct competitive motive: a faculty member in this building filed a competing grant two days earlier citing a dataset with the same parameters as the stolen drive. Priya’s library booking, Marcus’s work order, Jamie’s access restriction, and Nadia’s 3:05 PM exit all account for every other suspect. Only Dr. Osei — a faculty member with cross-wing access, working in the same research area, who filed a competing grant two days before the theft — matches the 3:22 PM fob tier, the pre-prepared sign-out form, and the motive.

title: The Director’s Cut
case: The memory card from director Anya Reyes’s personal camera — containing private behind-the-scenes footage of an unreleased film — disappeared from the production trailer on Meridian Studio’s lot between 2 PM and 4 PM.

suspects.name: Kenji
suspects.alibi: I was at camera base camp all afternoon troubleshooting a lens issue with my assistants. We didn’t wrap until 4:30.

suspects.name: Blythe
suspects.alibi: I was in Anya’s scheduling meeting from 1:45 PM. It ran long — we didn’t get out until 3:30. I never left the production building.

suspects.name: Cruz
suspects.alibi: I was at the props cage running inventory all afternoon. We had a big count before the next shooting day. My assistant was with me the whole time.

suspects.name: Simone
suspects.alibi: I was touring the set for a feature piece. My escort walked me to the trailer zone and said he’d be right back. I waited there while he went to coordinate something.

suspects.name: Hector
suspects.alibi: I was at the lot entry gate all day. That’s my post. I don’t leave it during a shoot day.

starter: The production trailer has a keypad entry code shared among approved crew. An insurance-required smart lock add-on logs all entries and exits: 2:07 PM (entry, 2-minute visit) and 2:41 PM (entry, 5-minute visit). Director Reyes was in a scheduling meeting in the main production building from 1:45 PM to 3:30 PM, confirmed by five attendees.

The missing card is a 256GB Sony Tough card with a red Sharpie dot on the label — Reyes’s personal marking. An entertainment news site posted an article at 6:45 PM that evening citing ‘exclusive on-set footage.’ The site’s traffic logs, obtained the following day, show the article draft was created at 3:15 PM — before any authorized distribution could have occurred.

The lot’s visitor log shows one visitor approved for a 2 PM to 4 PM window with access to the production trailer zone. All visitors are escorted; the escort signed the visitor in at 2:05 PM then returned to his department. The visitor’s transponder — all lot visitors carry one for safety tracking — logged a continuous signal at the trailer from 2:05 PM until 4:12 PM, when the badge was returned at the gate. The smart lock’s first entry at 2:07 PM falls 120 seconds after the visitor’s transponder first appears at the trailer.

exonerations.kenji: Kenji’s department radio check-ins place him at camera base camp at 2:15, 2:30, and 3:00 PM. Two camera assistants confirm he was troubleshooting a lens issue the entire afternoon and never left the area.
exonerations.blythe: Blythe was in the scheduling meeting with Reyes from 1:45 PM to 3:30 PM, confirmed by five attendees and Reyes’s calendar. Her phone’s location data shows no movement from the production building during that window.
exonerations.cruz: Cruz signed a props inventory sheet at 2:00 PM and again at 3:00 PM from the props cage, a separate building with its own keypad log. The inventory is co-signed by the props assistant, who was with Cruz continuously.
exonerations.hector: The lot entry gate has continuous camera coverage. Footage confirms Hector did not leave his post between 12 PM and 5 PM.

reveal: Clue 3 establishes that the sole lot visitor was unescorted in the trailer zone from 2:05 PM onward — their transponder shows a continuous signal at the trailer, and the smart lock’s 2:07 PM entry falls exactly 120 seconds after the transponder’s first ping. Clue 1 confirms the trailer was accessed at 2:07 and 2:41 PM during Reyes’s confirmed absence. Clue 2 shows the article draft was created at 3:15 PM, while the visitor’s badge was still active on the lot — the footage went from the trailer to a news draft in under 90 minutes. Only Simone, the entertainment journalist, had both the motive to publish the footage and the confirmed unescorted access to the trailer zone. Kenji’s radio check-ins, Blythe’s meeting record, Cruz’s co-signed inventory, and Hector’s gate camera account for everyone else. Simone was alone at the trailer at the moment of both entries and the only person whose profession explains why the footage ended up in a news draft that afternoon.

title: The Saturday Shortfall
case: The Riverside Saturday Market’s weekly cash box — $1,200 in vendor fees collected that morning — disappeared from market manager Audrey’s booth between 10 AM and noon. The locked metal box was found empty.

suspects.name: Beau
suspects.role: Flower Vendor, Booth Adjacent to Manager’s
suspects.alibi: I was at my booth all morning. Saturdays are my biggest sales day — I had customers constantly and never left.

suspects.name: Celeste
suspects.role: Nonprofit Coordinator, Donation Table Two Booths Over
suspects.alibi: I was at my table all morning with two volunteers collecting for the food bank. We had a good crowd.

suspects.name: Diego
suspects.alibi: I helped vendors set up early, then did general rounds — checking booth arrangements and handling small issues. I know this market inside out.

suspects.name: Wren
suspects.role: New Vendor, Artisan Soap, First Week at the Market
suspects.alibi: I was at my booth the whole time. This is my first market and I was nervous — I barely moved from my spot.

suspects.name: Phillip
suspects.role: Volunteer, Market Information Table at Entrance
suspects.alibi: I staffed the information table at the main entrance from 9 AM to close. Visitors ask me for directions constantly.

starter: The cash box is stored in a locked metal container under the counter of the manager’s booth. The only key hangs on a hook behind the counter — not visible from outside, but known to anyone who has regularly worked the manager’s station. A vendor’s Instagram photo taken for their own display, timestamped 10:34 AM, captured the manager’s booth in the background with the cash box visibly intact under the counter.

At 11:45 AM, a produce vendor reported that ‘the usual market staff guy’ had told her Audrey was needed at the front office immediately for a permit problem. Audrey left her booth unattended and walked to the market entrance — finding no one. She was gone 22 minutes. Riverside Saturday Market has no front office; it operates entirely from the manager’s booth.

The market’s buy-back log — a spiral notebook kept at the manager’s booth — records cash paid to vendors for end-of-market surplus. The last entry before the theft was discovered reads ‘11:50 AM, buy-back — $40.’ It was written during the window when Audrey was gone. Audrey confirms she made no buy-backs that morning, and the handwriting does not match her earlier entries in the notebook.

exonerations.beau: The 10:34 AM Instagram photo shows Beau actively arranging flowers at their booth. A neighboring vendor confirmed Beau never left their station for more than two minutes the entire morning.
exonerations.celeste: Celeste’s two volunteers confirm she was present and visible at the donation table from 9:30 AM until after the theft was discovered. Celeste has volunteered at this market for three years but has never worked the manager’s booth and does not know the key location.
exonerations.wren: Wren had never worked the manager’s booth before this market and had no knowledge of the key location or buy-back log. At least three customers photographed Wren at their soap display between 11:30 AM and noon.
exonerations.phillip: Phillip staffed the information table from 9 AM alongside two other volunteers, both of whom confirm he was there the entire morning. The table is at the opposite end of the market from the manager’s booth.

reveal: Clue 2 establishes that someone impersonated market staff to lure Audrey away using a invented errand — invoking a ‘front office’ that doesn’t exist. Only someone deeply familiar with the market’s operations would know Audrey would respond to a permit problem, yet miscalculate that she’d accept the ‘front office’ fiction once she was already walking. Clue 1 confirms the cash box was present at 10:34 AM; Clue 3 shows a forged buy-back entry at 11:50 AM, written during the precise 22-minute window Audrey was gone. Whoever wrote that entry knew where the notebook lived, knew the buy-back procedure, and could access the booth unchallenged. Diego — the part-time assistant who regularly works the market, knows the manager’s booth layout, and knows the key hook — is the only person who fits. Beau never left their booth, Celeste has never worked the manager’s station, Wren is a first-week outsider, and Phillip was at the entrance all morning.

title: The Appraiser’s Angle
case: A rare 1920s pocket watch appraised at $8,000 went missing from Thornwood Antiques during a private appraisal evening. The locked display case where it had been stored was found empty when the shop owner prepared to close.

suspects.name: Harriet
suspects.alibi: I hosted the event in the main showroom all evening — welcoming guests and answering questions about the collection. I was visible to everyone the whole time.

suspects.name: Douglas
suspects.role: Professional Antique Appraiser, Hired for the Evening
suspects.alibi: I moved through the showroom systematically, assessing pieces and taking notes. I examined the pocket watch early in the evening and moved on to other items.

suspects.name: Minette
suspects.role: Private Collector, Attended as a Guest
suspects.alibi: My husband and I browsed the collection for most of the evening. The pocket watch was beautiful — we admired it through the case glass.

suspects.name: Freddie
suspects.alibi: I was at the refreshment table by the door all evening. It was my first big event here and I didn’t want to mess anything up, so I stayed put.

suspects.name: Opal
suspects.role: Insurance Adjuster, Photographing Items for Policy Update
suspects.alibi: I photographed items for the insurance inventory, then left around 7:30. I had an early morning and didn’t want to stay the full event.

starter: The pocket watch was in a locked glass case in the front room. The only case key is kept in Harriet’s desk drawer in the back office — not publicized. A motion sensor in the back office, installed by the insurance company, logged one activation at 7:42 PM. The event was held in the main showroom; no one had any legitimate reason to enter the back office during the event.

A jeweler’s loupe was found on the counter beside the empty display case after the event. It is not Thornwood’s — their loupe is engraved with the shop name. This loupe has no markings but has a strip of black foam tape on the handle, a common modification among professional appraisers for improved grip. Harriet has never seen it before.

The watch’s manufacturer, Elgin, serialized all movements. This watch’s serial number places it in an 1921 production run of 200 units. Two weeks before the event, a post appeared on a specialty horology forum: ‘seeking reproduction commission — specific 1921 Elgin movement, reference photos available on request.’ The account was created that same week and has since been deleted. The posting account’s forum signature read: ‘professional antique appraisal services.’

exonerations.harriet: Three guests, including Minette and her husband, confirm Harriet was visible in the showroom throughout the evening and was never absent for more than a minute. She had no motive to steal from her own locked case.
exonerations.minette: Minette arrived with her husband and remained in the showroom with him all evening. She had never visited Thornwood before, had no knowledge of the key’s location in the back office, and carries no professional appraisal tools.
exonerations.freddie: Harriet and Minette both confirm Freddie was stationed at the refreshment table by the front door all evening. He is new to the job and unfamiliar with the shop’s storage and key systems.
exonerations.opal: Opal left the event at 7:30 PM, confirmed by the building’s parking-lot camera showing her car exiting at 7:31 PM — eleven minutes before the back-office motion sensor activated at 7:42 PM.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes that someone entered the back office at 7:42 PM — where the case key is kept — with no legitimate reason to be there during the event. Clue 2 places a professional appraiser’s own tool at the scene: the foam-taped loupe is a working appraiser’s personal modification, not something a casual guest carries. Clue 3 shows advance knowledge: a forum commission request for this exact watch’s 1921 serial run appeared two weeks before the event, posted by an account whose signature advertises professional appraisal services. Harriet stayed in the showroom, Minette had no knowledge of the back office or tools, Freddie never left the refreshment table, and Opal departed eleven minutes before the back office was accessed. Douglas — the professional appraiser with the trade connections and the tools — pre-commissioned the reproduction, located the case key during the appraisal event, and left his own loupe behind in the process.

title: The Closing Time Job
case: The Friday cash deposit at Rosewood Bakery — $2,400 in day-of sales — went missing from the office safe. Owner June confirmed the money was in the safe at 6 PM before leaving; by the time she returned at 9 PM to close out, the safe was empty.

suspects.name: Petra
suspects.alibi: I finished cleanup and left around 6:55. I went straight to my sister’s place for dinner — we were on a video call most of the evening catching up.

suspects.name: Cal
suspects.alibi: I made a flour delivery around 6:30 and was in and out in five minutes. I had four more stops on my route after Rosewood.

suspects.name: Yusuf
suspects.alibi: I did a routine inspection from 7 PM to 8:05 PM — front of house, prep kitchen, equipment. I filed my report that same night.

suspects.name: Margaux
suspects.role: Former Bakery Employee and Close Friend of Petra
suspects.alibi: I forgot my jacket in the break room and swung by around 7:30 to grab it. I was in and out in under five minutes.

suspects.name: Felix
suspects.alibi: I fixed the espresso machine in the early afternoon and was done by 4:45. I packed up and left. Three people saw me go.

starter: The office safe uses a four-digit combination known only to June and Petra. The combination was changed three weeks ago when a former employee left. The safe was found closed but unlocked — the dial had been turned through the correct sequence and the door simply pushed shut. No signs of forced entry.

The staff entrance uses a key-fob reader. The fob log for the evening shows: Cal in/out at 6:28/6:33 PM, Petra out at 6:55 PM, one staff fob entry at 7:34 PM, and June’s fob at 8:22 PM on her early return. The 7:34 PM entry belongs to a staff fob issued to an employee who left three weeks ago and was never asked to return it.

Yusuf’s inspection report documents only the front counter, display cases, prep kitchen, and equipment — the back office is not mentioned. Cal’s dispatch GPS log shows his truck leaving Rosewood at 6:33 PM and arriving at the next stop at 6:41 PM with no detour. The 7:34 PM fob entry is the only access to the building between Cal’s departure and June’s return at 8:22 PM — a 109-minute window with one person inside.

exonerations.petra: Petra’s fob confirms she exited at 6:55 PM. Her phone records show an incoming video call from her sister at 7:10 PM that lasted 83 minutes. She was not in the building during the theft window.
exonerations.cal: Cal’s delivery route is GPS-tracked by dispatch. He was at Rosewood from 6:26 to 6:33 PM, then continuously on his route with no detour. He was never issued a staff fob and has no knowledge of the safe combination.
exonerations.yusuf: Yusuf’s inspection report and the kitchen prep camera both confirm he remained in the front-of-house and kitchen areas for his entire visit. He never entered the hallway leading to the back office.
exonerations.felix: Felix completed his repair at 4:45 PM and left before the close of business. The customer-entry log shows no re-entry afterward, and he was never issued a staff fob.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes that the safe combination was entered correctly — no forced entry — which means the thief knew the four-digit code, changed three weeks ago and shared only between June and Petra. Clue 2 identifies the 7:34 PM fob entry as belonging to a staff fob issued to an employee who left three weeks ago — exactly when the combination was last changed. Clue 3 accounts for every other person in the building: Yusuf stayed in the kitchen area, Cal’s GPS confirms he left at 6:33, and the 7:34 PM entry is the sole access in the 109-minute gap. Petra was on a video call from 7:10 PM and never returned. Margaux, Petra’s closest friend, left the bakery three weeks ago with an unreturned fob — and almost certainly learned the new safe code from Petra in casual conversation. Her ‘forgotten jacket’ was a pretext to be alone in the bakery with a combination she wasn’t supposed to know.

title: The Pre-Sale Swap
case: A Victorian diamond brooch listed for auction at Hartwell & Sons was found to be a high-quality replica at final staging on auction day. The original — authenticated and valued at $45,000 — had been swapped at some point in the 48 hours before the sale.

suspects.name: Constance
suspects.alibi: I authenticated the brooch on May 12 using our GIA-certified equipment. It was genuine. I haven’t been back to the storage room since.

suspects.name: Reginald
suspects.role: Consigner, Original Owner of the Brooch
suspects.alibi: I came in on the afternoon of May 13 for a final preview look. The brooch looked exactly as I remembered it — I didn’t handle it.

suspects.name: Lark
suspects.alibi: I handled the intake on May 12 and brought the brooch out for staging on May 14. That’s when I found the replica. I raised the alarm immediately.

suspects.name: Petra
suspects.role: Registered Bidder, Known Interest in Victorian Jewelry
suspects.alibi: I attended the public preview gallery on May 13 and admired the brooch through the display glass. I never went anywhere near the storage area.

suspects.name: Emerson
suspects.role: Independent Conservator, Hired to Assess Condition
suspects.alibi: I visited on the morning of May 13 to complete a condition report on the brooch. I examined the piece closely and submitted my findings. Routine work.

starter: The auction house’s secure viewing room requires a keypad code. The access log shows five entries between authentication and staging: Lark (9:00 AM May 12 — intake), Constance (2:00 PM May 12 — authentication), Emerson (10:00 AM May 13 — condition assessment), Reginald (3:00 PM May 13 — consigner preview), and Lark (9:00 AM May 14 — staging, when the replica was discovered).

The replica is a high-quality paste-and-gilt reproduction. A Victorian jewelry collector forum post from late April advertised a commission: ‘seeking skilled reproduction of a specific Victorian diamond cluster brooch — reference photos available on request.’ The account was created that week and has since been deleted. The posting account’s signature read: ‘independent conservation and period assessment services.’

Hartwell’s intake photograph, taken by Lark on May 12, shows the brooch’s principal stone clearly. Constance’s authentication report — recorded directly to calibrated GIA equipment and timestamped — logs the principal stone at 4.7 mm. Emerson’s condition report, submitted after the May 13 assessment, records the principal stone at 4.2 mm. A gemologist comparing both reports concluded that the 4.2 mm measurement matches the replica and cannot be reconciled with the original stone documented on intake.

exonerations.constance: Constance’s digitally logged GIA measurements from May 12 match the original brooch in Lark’s intake photograph — the swap had not yet occurred at the time of her assessment. The access log shows no return visit by Constance after May 12.
exonerations.reginald: The display case is equipped with a magnetic sensor that logs every opening. During Reginald’s 3:00 PM visit on May 13, the sensor recorded no opening event — he viewed the brooch through the glass only, consistent with his account.
exonerations.lark: Lark’s only two access events bookend the timeline: intake before authentication and staging when the replica was found. As a junior assistant with no appraisal credentials, Lark would have no channel to commission a high-quality period reproduction.
exonerations.petra: Registered bidders are not granted keypad access to the secure viewing room. The access log contains no entry for Petra. She attended only the public preview gallery.

reveal: Clue 1 shows Emerson was alone with the brooch during the May 13 morning assessment — the only access between Constance’s authenticated measurement on May 12 and Reginald’s case-closed preview that afternoon. Clue 3 catches the critical discrepancy: Emerson’s condition report records the principal stone at 4.2 mm, but Constance’s calibrated GIA log from the day before reads 4.7 mm, matching the intake photograph. Emerson measured the replica — either because someone else had already swapped it, or because Emerson made the swap and then documented what was there. Clue 2 eliminates the first possibility: a forum commission request for a reproduction of this exact brooch — signed with ‘conservation and period assessment services’ — was posted in late April, before Emerson’s visit was even scheduled. Constance’s digital log, Reginald’s case sensor, Lark’s limited access, and Petra’s exclusion from storage all eliminate the other four. Emerson commissioned the replica weeks in advance, made the swap during the condition assessment, and submitted measurements that matched the fake — inadvertently writing the confession into the official record.

title: The Members Preview
case: A 16th-century silver reliquary disappeared from the Halverton Museum's medieval collection during a members-only preview evening. The piece was confirmed present at 6 PM during the curator's final walkthrough; by 9 PM it was gone.

suspects.name: Orla
suspects.alibi: I was in the registry office processing end-of-day intake paperwork. I didn't go near the medieval wing at all.

suspects.name: Theo
suspects.alibi: I led tours of the medieval wing at 6:30 and 7:45. I was with guests the entire evening and filed my incident report before leaving.

suspects.name: Petra
suspects.alibi: I attended the preview and admired the reliquary from about three feet away — it's behind glass. I left around 8 PM.

suspects.name: Dara
suspects.alibi: I completed my full patrol of the medieval wing at 7:15. Everything was in order. I documented it in the patrol log.

suspects.name: Wren
suspects.alibi: My crew was set up in the atrium from 5 PM to close. I was managing the buffet and bar service the entire evening.

starter: The museum's infrared motion system logs movement independently in each gallery. Medieval wing log: Theo (6:15, 6:33, 7:31, 7:47, 8:17 PM), Dara (7:15 PM). The reliquary's display case is equipped with a magnetic contact sensor — it registered an opening at 8:19 PM. No tour was scheduled after 8 PM.

The reliquary was replaced with a weighted bundle of museum-grade archival linen — the same material used exclusively in the collection's storage and transit supply room. Staff have access to this supply room; attending members do not. The case was re-locked after the swap.

Theo's end-of-evening incident report, filed at 8:45 PM, notes 'nothing unusual observed in the medieval wing.' A review of the infrared log shows his 8:17 PM entry — after the last scheduled tour — lasted eleven minutes, ending at 8:28 PM. The case sensor opened at 8:19 PM. Theo filed for personal bankruptcy three months ago.

exonerations.orla: The registry office is in the administrative wing with its own keypad access log. Orla's badge shows continuous presence from 5:45 PM to 9:30 PM with no gaps. She has no key to medieval gallery storage and no access to the transit supply room.
exonerations.petra: Member guests do not receive staff-level gallery access. Petra's member badge logs an exit at 8:03 PM — sixteen minutes before the case sensor opened. The infrared system shows no member-tier presence in the medieval wing after 8:05 PM.
exonerations.dara: Dara's 7:15 PM patrol predates the 8:19 PM case opening by over an hour. The patrol log — cross-referenced with the building's elevator system — places Dara in the east-wing stairwell at 8:19 PM, assisting a member who had lost their phone. A member confirmed the interaction.
exonerations.wren: Wren's catering crew operated entirely in the atrium and adjacent service corridor. The atrium camera shows Wren continuously from 7:30 PM onward managing service. Wren has no access to the medieval gallery or the transit supply room.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes an unscheduled return to the medieval wing at 8:17 PM — after all tours had ended — with the display case opening two minutes later at 8:19 PM. Clue 2 shows the reliquary was replaced with museum-grade archival linen, a material only staff can access, and that the case was re-locked afterward — indicating someone who knew the layout and supplies. Clue 3 closes the trap: Theo's own incident report claims nothing unusual, directly contradicting the infrared log that places him in the wing at 8:17 PM; his eleven-minute stay brackets the 8:19 case opening exactly; and his recent bankruptcy filing gives him financial motive. Petra's badge exit, Dara's stairwell log, Orla's registry records, and Wren's catering camera eliminate every other suspect. Theo used his legitimate tour access and knowledge of the supply room to stage a swap — then wrote a false report to ensure no one would look his way.

title: The Vintage Vault
case: Six bottles of 1961 Pétrus Bordeaux — valued at approximately $60,000 — disappeared from the locked wine cellar at Ashworth Estate. The bottles were confirmed present during a private dinner on the evening of May 15; by noon on May 16 they were gone.

suspects.name: Barnaby
suspects.alibi: I did my usual morning walkthrough at 9 AM. The cellar was locked when I checked — I didn't go in.

suspects.name: Claudette
suspects.alibi: I was in the kitchen all morning prepping brunch. I don't even have a key to the wine cellar.

suspects.name: Miles
suspects.alibi: I complimented the Pétrus at dinner, certainly. But I was in my guest suite from about midnight until noon — I had no reason to get up early.

suspects.name: Isadora
suspects.alibi: I clean the main house, not the cellar. I don't have a key, and I was doing linens and bathrooms all morning.

suspects.name: Rupert
suspects.alibi: I came down for breakfast around 10 and went for a walk along the garden path after. I was back by 11:30.

starter: The wine cellar uses a traditional mortise lock with two keys: one held by Barnaby (engraved 'EK-1') and one stored in the butler's pantry key cabinet. The key cabinet ledger shows one sign-out at 11:47 PM on May 15, returned at 12:14 AM on May 16, signed with an illegible initial. The cabinet's four-digit access code is known only to Barnaby and the Ashworth family.

A wine shipping specialist identified the six missing bottles the following morning as matching a consignment that arrived at a specialist auction house sixty miles away. The consignment was dropped off in person at 9:30 AM by an individual matching Rupert's description, driving a vehicle registered to the estate. The auction intake form lists the consigner as 'R. Ashworth.'

GPS history retrieved from the estate's second vehicle — a dark Range Rover kept in the utility outbuilding — shows a trip departing at 8:52 AM and returning at 11:18 AM, with a sixty-minute stop at the auction house address. Rupert's stated return time of 11:30 AM is consistent with walking in from the outbuilding, which sits at the end of the garden path.

exonerations.barnaby: Barnaby's key — engraved 'EK-1' — was on his keychain when examined by investigators. The cabinet's 'EK-2' copy is the one that was signed out. Barnaby also has no familial claim on the estate's wine and no known financial motive.
exonerations.claudette: Claudette has no key to the cellar and her name does not appear in the cabinet ledger. Kitchen records show a 7 AM grocery delivery she signed for; witnesses confirm her continuous presence in the kitchen from 7:30 AM onward.
exonerations.miles: Miles is a weekend guest without access to the key cabinet, which requires a family-known code. His guest room's smart thermostat log shows continuous occupancy from 11:45 PM to 10:15 AM — he was asleep through the overnight cellar access window.
exonerations.isadora: Isadora has no key to the cellar and does not know the cabinet code. She does not drive and has no vehicle, making a 9:30 AM auction drop-off sixty miles away impossible.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes that the cabinet's second key was removed at 11:47 PM and returned at 12:14 AM — a thirty-minute window in which six heavy bottles could be moved to a waiting vehicle — and that only Barnaby or an Ashworth family member could operate the cabinet. Clue 2 is the decisive link: the missing bottles appeared at auction that morning, consigned under 'R. Ashworth,' dropped off in an estate vehicle by someone matching Rupert's description. Clue 3 closes the alibi gap: the Range Rover's GPS confirms the 8:52 AM departure, a sixty-minute stop at the exact auction house address, and an 11:18 AM return — placing the vehicle at the auction house at 9:30 AM. Barnaby's engraved key rules him out; Miles's thermostat record, Claudette's kitchen witnesses, and Isadora's access and transportation constraints eliminate the rest. Rupert used the family cabinet code overnight to move the bottles, then personally consigned them at auction the next morning under his own name.

title: The Override
case: A 3D-printed prototype of a classified drone navigation component disappeared from the secured cabinet in Lab 3 at Varro Defense Concepts. The prototype was signed in at 8 AM and was missing by the 5 PM end-of-day check.

suspects.name: Dex
suspects.alibi: I was in back-to-back meetings all day. I didn't touch the prototype.

suspects.name: Yara
suspects.alibi: I ran morning tests, signed the prototype back into the cabinet at noon, and went to lunch. I didn't go back into the lab after that.

suspects.name: Samir
suspects.alibi: I was in the server room all day running a critical storage migration. You can check the system logs.

suspects.name: Lorraine
suspects.alibi: I was with the VP all day — meetings, calls, lunch. I don't have clearance for Lab 3.

suspects.name: Conrad
suspects.alibi: I patrol the building and monitor the access logs. I check the hallways, not the labs themselves.

starter: The prototype cabinet requires a four-digit combination known only to Dex and Yara, plus an RFID card scan to open the door. The RFID log shows: Yara at 8:10 AM (check-out) and 12:02 PM (return), and a third entry at 2:47 PM where the reader logged 'ADMIN' — the system's fallback display when the IT master override card is used.

Varro's network security system flagged an unusual outbound transfer at 2:51 PM: a CAD file package matching the prototype's specification was sent to an external address via personal webmail, accessed from the server room's dedicated admin workstation. The sending IP resolves exclusively to that workstation.

Conrad's GPS-timestamped patrol log places him in the Lab 3 corridor at 2:40 PM and again at 3:15 PM — he did not enter the lab. Yara's badge shows no lab re-entry after 12:02 PM. Dex's conference room badge confirms continuous presence in meetings from 9:00 AM to 4:30 PM. The IT master override card is issued to exactly one employee at Varro Defense Concepts.

exonerations.dex: Conference room badge records confirm Dex was in meetings continuously from 9:00 AM to 4:30 PM without interruption. His engineer RFID card is distinct from the override card; the 2:47 PM entry was not logged as his card.
exonerations.yara: Yara's access badge shows lab entry at 8:08 AM and exit at 12:02 PM, with no re-entry. She does not hold an IT master override card and has no server room access.
exonerations.lorraine: Lorraine's access badge is restricted to executive floors and common areas — it would be rejected at the Lab 3 RFID reader. The VP's calendar confirms she was in or adjacent to his office from 8:30 AM through 5 PM.
exonerations.conrad: Conrad's body camera — which GPS-timestamps all patrol footage — recorded him in the hallway at 2:40 PM, not inside the lab. He carries a facilities pass, not an IT override card, and has no server room access.

reveal: Clue 1 identifies the 2:47 PM cabinet access as made with the IT master override card — the RFID system's 'ADMIN' fallback is triggered only by that card, which is issued to one person. Clue 2 places a file transfer of the prototype's CAD specifications at 2:51 PM — four minutes later — from the server room's admin workstation to an external address, meaning whoever used the override card in the lab was back in the server room within four minutes. Clue 3 closes every other door: Dex's conference badge is continuous, Yara never re-entered after noon, Lorraine's badge would be rejected at the lab reader, and Conrad's body camera places him only in the hallway. The IT master override card belongs to Samir alone. He used it to open the secured cabinet at 2:47 PM, took or photographed the prototype, returned to the server room, and transmitted the CAD file at 2:51 PM — concealing the act inside a day-long migration that gave him a plausible reason to be at the admin workstation all afternoon.

title: The Hermetic Case
case: A 17th-century illuminated manuscript — on loan from a private collection and valued at $280,000 — disappeared from the Fenwick University Rare Books Room. The piece was present during a 9 AM research session and was found missing at 2 PM.

suspects.name: Agatha
suspects.alibi: I was at the reference desk all morning. I let the researchers in, verified their IDs, and monitored the room. I held the only key to the hermetic case.

suspects.name: Brennan
suspects.alibi: I worked with the manuscript from 9 to 11:30, then left. I followed every protocol — white gloves, no photography, materials kept flat.

suspects.name: Orin
suspects.alibi: One of my crew fixed a pipe leak in the restroom adjacent to the rare books room between 10 and 11 AM. I walked by briefly to check on the work.

suspects.name: Cassie
suspects.alibi: I gave a donor tour of the library building that morning. We were in the special collections hallway around 10:30 — I pointed out the rare books room through the window.

suspects.name: Hollis
suspects.role: Independent Conservator, On-Site for Condition Assessment
suspects.alibi: I was engaged to assess two other items in the rare books room from 1 to 2 PM. Standard condition work. I left when I'd finished the two pieces.

starter: The rare books room uses a sign-in ledger monitored at Agatha's desk. Morning log: Brennan (9:00–11:32 AM), Hollis (1:05–1:58 PM). A separate climate sensor logs temperature and humidity in the room; it recorded a four-minute humidity excursion at 1:47 PM, consistent with the hermetic document case being opened.

The hermetic case requires a key held only by Agatha. After Hollis's session, the case was found unlocked and empty. Hollis's conservation kit, examined by investigators, contained a custom foam-lined extraction tray sized to within two millimeters of the manuscript's dimensions — not a standard conservator's tool, and not required for the two items officially scheduled for assessment.

A rare books dealer in Geneva received an email inquiry at 3:14 PM that day from a Hollis Whitmore asking whether they handled 'early 17th-century devotional manuscripts of northern European origin' and whether a piece could be 'quietly assessed for private placement.' The dealer forwarded the inquiry to Interpol two days later after seeing a description of the stolen manuscript circulated online.

exonerations.agatha: Agatha is the sole authorized key-holder; the hermetic case lock shows no sign of forced entry, confirming a key was used. As the room's custodian, Agatha would have no reason to steal from her own collection and would bear direct professional liability for the loss.
exonerations.brennan: Brennan signed out at 11:32 AM — two and a half hours before Hollis's session and the 1:47 PM humidity excursion. The ledger records no re-entry, and the access door camera confirms Brennan did not return.
exonerations.orin: Orin was observed only in the hallway outside the restroom; he does not appear in the room's sign-in ledger. The access door requires librarian sign-in, and Orin's name was never added.
exonerations.cassie: The donor tour remained in the hallway and common areas. Cassie's visitor lanyard does not grant access to the controlled research environment; she is not in the room's ledger and could not have operated the hermetic case lock.

reveal: Clue 1 places the key event at 1:47 PM — a humidity excursion consistent with the hermetic case being opened during Hollis's session, the only access event that afternoon. Clue 2 shows Hollis arrived with a purpose-built extraction tray sized precisely to the manuscript — not needed for the two officially scheduled items, indicating premeditated preparation before the visit. Clue 3 closes the window: Hollis was emailing a Geneva dealer about a northern European devotional manuscript at 3:14 PM, barely ninety minutes after leaving the room, using language about 'quiet placement' that the dealer later connected to the theft. Brennan's exit two and a half hours prior, Orin's hallway-only presence, Cassie's lanyard restriction, and Agatha's position as the key-holder all eliminate the other four. Hollis came equipped with a custom tray, opened the hermetic case at 1:47 PM, and was already reaching out to international buyers before the theft was even discovered.

title: The Cold Room
case: A vacuum-sealed container of Beluga caviar — valued at $4,200 — disappeared from the walk-in cold room at the Aldgate restaurant. The caviar was inventoried at 3 PM and found missing during the 9 PM dinner service check.

suspects.name: Gwen
suspects.alibi: I was running dinner prep from 3 PM onward. I went into the cold room briefly before that but not after.

suspects.name: Fabio
suspects.alibi: I was prepping the pasta section all afternoon. I went into the cold room twice for stock — both times with the garde-manger cook right there.

suspects.name: Lisette
suspects.alibi: I don't go into the kitchen unless there's a guest complaint. I was at the host stand all evening managing reservations.

suspects.name: Theo
suspects.alibi: I made my last delivery at 4:30. I bring everything to the loading dock and leave it there — I'm in and out in five minutes.

suspects.name: Marlow
suspects.alibi: My station is separate from the savory kitchen. I use the cold room for dairy items — I was in twice between 4 and 6 PM, both short visits.

starter: The cold room has a keypad entry system that logs each badge separately. Full log for the 3 PM to 9 PM window: Gwen (3:05 in, 3:12 out), Fabio (3:42 in/3:55 out, 4:10 in/4:18 out), Theo (4:33 in, 4:38 out), Marlow (4:13 in/4:21 out, 5:47 in/5:52 out, 6:31 in/6:39 out). The caviar container — a sealed case with its own padlock — was confirmed present during Fabio's 3:55 entry and missing at the 9 PM check.

The loading dock camera covers the rear entrance clearly. Theo's delivery van is visible from 4:30 to 4:35 PM; he is seen unloading produce crates at the dock threshold and never entering past the loading dock door. Marlow reported two cold room visits between 4 and 6 PM; the keypad log records three.

Text messages retrieved from Marlow's phone show a conversation two days prior with a contact saved as 'PD' arranging to 'bring the usual Saturday item, extra this time.' A search of Marlow's locker found a personal insulated grocery bag — the type rated for perishable transport — folded inside a pastry apron. Beluga caviar remains stable for several hours in an insulated carrier.

exonerations.gwen: Gwen's log shows entry at 3:05 PM and exit at 3:12 PM — before Fabio's 3:55 confirmation that the caviar was still present. Kitchen cameras show Gwen at the pass station continuously from 3:15 PM onward with no cold room return.
exonerations.fabio: Fabio's 4:10 PM entry was witnessed by the garde-manger cook, who was with him the entire visit. His last cold room entry was at 4:18 PM — over two hours before Marlow's undisclosed 6:31 PM visit.
exonerations.lisette: Lisette's keycard is programmed for front-of-house areas only; the cold room reader would reject it. Kitchen staff confirmed she did not enter the kitchen during the evening.
exonerations.theo: The loading dock camera confirms Theo remained at the dock threshold handling produce crates and never entered the interior kitchen. His badge grants access only to the receiving area, not the cold room.

reveal: Clue 1 narrows the theft window to after Fabio's 3:55 PM confirmation, leaving Marlow's three logged entries as the only cold room access in the relevant period. Clue 2 catches the discrepancy directly: Marlow reported two visits but the keypad recorded three — the undisclosed 6:31 PM entry lasted eight minutes, the longest of any visit that afternoon. The dock camera eliminates Theo entirely. Clue 3 completes the picture: a pre-arranged agreement to 'bring the usual Saturday item, extra this time,' plus a personal insulated bag found in Marlow's locker — the exact tool needed to transport refrigerated goods off the premises. Gwen's 3:12 PM exit precedes the crime window, Fabio's last visit was over two hours earlier and witnessed, Lisette's keycard is restricted, and Theo never entered the kitchen. The unreported third visit — eight minutes long, on the evening of a pre-arranged handoff — is when Marlow removed the caviar.

title: The House Party
case: A matching pair of engraved silver dueling pistols from 1812 — appraised at $35,000 — disappeared from the locked study at Dunvale Manor during a weekend house party. The pistols were present during an informal display at 11 AM; by 6 PM they were gone.

suspects.name: Helena
suspects.alibi: They're my pistols and my study. I have no reason to steal my own property — and I'd simply move them if I wanted them elsewhere.

suspects.name: Edmund
suspects.alibi: I admired the pistols in the morning but left with everyone else. I was playing croquet on the lawn all afternoon.

suspects.name: Rosalind
suspects.alibi: I was in the drawing room reading from noon until tea. I never went back to the study.

suspects.name: Padraig
suspects.alibi: I served lunch, cleared the table, and managed the afternoon tea service. I don't carry a key to the study — that's the family's domain.

suspects.name: Fern
suspects.alibi: I photographed the pistols and other pieces as part of a documentation commission. I finished by 11 AM and left the manor by noon.

starter: The study has a mortise lock with two keys: one held by Helena and one kept in the butler's pantry key cabinet, which requires a four-digit code known only to Helena and her immediate family. The estate key logbook shows the cabinet key was signed out at 11:45 AM and returned at 12:10 PM. The sign-out line bears an illegible initial — not Padraig's signature.

Fern's photography commission was arranged through a third-party agency. The agency's client record shows the commission was requested by an individual who specified 'complete close-up documentation of all engraving, hallmarks, and proof marks' — an unusually technical requirement for a general estate documentation job, and precisely the information needed to authenticate or privately broker antique firearms.

The estate parking log shows Fern's hired car departing at 12:14 PM. A customs declaration filed at Dover Ferry Terminal the following morning lists 'antique decorative items' declared by a consignee whose contact address matches the photography agency's client record. Fern's contact sheet, left with Helena after the shoot, documents the pistols only through 10:52 AM — four minutes before the scheduled final shot, suggesting the session ended abruptly.

exonerations.helena: Helena holds the primary study key at all times and controls the cabinet code. She had no reason to use the secondary key, and the pistols are fully insured in her name. The insurance company confirmed no prior claim irregularities.
exonerations.edmund: Edmund does not know the cabinet code and did not appear in the key logbook. The manor's croquet lawn is visible from the drawing room windows; Rosalind's account places him on the lawn throughout the afternoon, corroborated by the groundskeeper.
exonerations.rosalind: Rosalind likewise does not know the cabinet code. The drawing room camera shows her in the reading armchair continuously from 12:10 PM to 4:45 PM.
exonerations.padraig: Padraig manages the key cabinet but his name does not appear on the sign-out line — meaning someone accessed the cabinet while he was occupied with lunch service. He has worked at the manor for nine years without incident and has no known motive.

reveal: Clue 1 shows the secondary study key was signed out at 11:45 AM by someone other than Padraig — while Fern was still ostensibly wrapping up work — and returned at 12:10 PM, four minutes before Fern's car left the estate. Clue 2 reveals the commission itself as suspicious: requesting hallmark and proof-mark close-ups goes beyond documentation into exactly the data needed to authenticate or broker firearms privately, suggesting the job was designed to give Fern legitimate access and a full photographic record of the pieces. Clue 3 connects three data points: Fern's 12:14 PM departure, a next-morning customs declaration listing antique decorative items from the agency's client address, and a contact sheet that cuts off four minutes early — consistent with Fern stopping photography to remove the pistols. Edmund's lawn alibi, Rosalind's drawing room camera, Padraig's management role, and Helena's ownership all eliminate the others. Fern used the lunch-service distraction to access the cabinet at 11:45, entered the study, and had the pistols in the car by 12:14 PM.

title: The Boardroom Breach
case: Confidential merger documents for the Aldric-Soto acquisition were photographed and leaked from the locked conference room at Brennan & Foley LLP. The documents were printed at 9 AM; an internal review at 3 PM found they had been disturbed, and the data surfaced at a hedge fund the following morning.

suspects.name: Victoria
suspects.role: Senior Partner, Lead on the Aldric-Soto File
suspects.alibi: I was in client meetings all morning and didn't return to the conference room until nearly noon. I noticed the documents had been moved when I came back.

suspects.name: Marcus
suspects.alibi: I printed the documents at 9 AM under Victoria's supervision and filed them in the conference room. I went straight to my desk after and didn't go back in.

suspects.name: Sloane
suspects.alibi: I've been prepping exhibits for days. I went in briefly to cross-check a tab sequence, then left. I have no reason to sabotage my own deal.

suspects.name: Piers
suspects.alibi: I was called in to fix the conference room's presentation screen. I was there about twenty minutes, around 11 AM.

suspects.name: Rhea
suspects.role: Opposing Counsel's Associate, Discovery Exchange Visit
suspects.alibi: I dropped off discovery boxes in the lobby at 10 AM. A receptionist escorted me the entire time — I never went past the lobby.

starter: The conference room requires a keycard programmed for authorized personnel only. Access log: Victoria (8:55 AM), Marcus (9:05 AM in, 9:22 AM out), Sloane (10:12 AM in, 10:40 AM out), Piers (11:05 AM in, 11:27 AM out), Victoria (11:50 AM — she noted the documents had been moved from their exact stacked position with colored exhibit tabs).

A secure IoT panel embedded in the conference room's printer detected a rapid-sequence image-capture event at 11:19 AM — a camera-type radio signature consistent with burst-mode photography, not a phone call. The event lasted 41 seconds and was logged between Piers's 11:05 entry and 11:27 exit.

Piers was placed through an external IT staffing firm. Three days before the engagement, a burner phone registered to the firm's commercial address received a call from a number linked to a hedge fund currently under SEC investigation for insider trading. Piers's work ticket — flagged as 'urgent' — was generated by an internal system account that does not correspond to any attorney at Brennan & Foley.

exonerations.victoria: Victoria's access was at 8:55 AM and 11:50 AM. She was in a client meeting from 10 AM to 11:45 AM, confirmed by the client and the reception visitor log. The documents were already disturbed when she returned at 11:50.
exonerations.marcus: Marcus's last access was at 9:22 AM — 117 minutes before the 11:19 AM capture event. The documents were intact when Sloane entered at 10:12, confirming nothing had been taken by 9:22.
exonerations.sloane: Sloane's exit at 10:40 AM precedes Piers's 11:05 entry by twenty-five minutes and the 11:19 AM capture event by thirty-nine minutes. Sloane's badge shows no re-entry to the conference room after 10:40.
exonerations.rhea: Rhea was escorted by a firm receptionist at all times during her 10 AM lobby visit and was never granted keycard access to any conference room. The visitor log confirms she exited the lobby by 10:20 AM.

reveal: Clue 1 places Piers alone in the conference room from 11:05 to 11:27 AM — after Sloane's departure and before Victoria's 11:50 return — as the only unaccounted visitor during the disturbance window. Clue 2 catches the photography at 11:19 AM: a 41-second burst-mode camera event logged by the room's IoT panel, occurring squarely in the middle of Piers's visit. Clue 3 supplies the motive chain: a hedge fund under SEC investigation called a burner phone at Piers's staffing firm three days before the engagement, and the work ticket was generated by a ghost account — not any attorney — suggesting Piers was deliberately placed. Victoria's client meeting, Marcus's 9:22 AM exit, Sloane's 10:40 departure, and Rhea's escorted lobby visit eliminate every other suspect. Piers was inserted into the firm on a manufactured IT ticket, photographed the merger documents in a 41-second burst at 11:19 AM, and walked out with the entire acquisition strategy while his access log made him look like a routine maintenance call.

title: The Replica
case: A 19th-century marine chronometer authenticated as having sailed aboard HMS Beagle — valued at $68,000 — disappeared from a locked display case at the Hartley Yacht Club and was replaced with a period-style facsimile. The original was confirmed present at 9:15 AM; the substitution was discovered at 4 PM.

suspects.name: Nora
suspects.alibi: I handled the piece during setup and locked the case at 9:15 AM after the morning viewing started. I haven't touched it since — I have the only key.

suspects.name: Flynn
suspects.alibi: I gave a lecture on naval timekeeping in the adjoining room from 11 AM to 12:30. I never entered the display area after the case was locked.

suspects.name: Odette
suspects.alibi: I photographed every item in the display room between 1 and 1:47 PM — standard documentation for the annual valuation. I don't handle the pieces.

suspects.name: Graham
suspects.alibi: I attended the morning viewing but left at 9:45 for a training run on the water. I was back at the club bar by noon.

suspects.name: Ivan
suspects.role: Licensed Locksmith, Called for a Faulty Office Drawer Lock
suspects.alibi: I fixed a drawer lock in the office adjacent to the display room between 2 and 3 PM. I was never inside the display room itself.

starter: The display case has a tamper-evident sensor log. Entries: Nora (9:02 AM open, 9:15 AM close/lock), Odette's access badge (1:03 PM open, 1:47 PM close — 44 minutes). No further openings were recorded. The facsimile found at 4 PM was produced using a period-appropriate brass casting, but its patina was applied with a chemical accelerant rather than accumulated naturally — a distinction identified by the conservator who examined it.

Odette's camera, submitted for evidence review, contains 87 photographs taken during her 44-minute session. Of these, 31 are close-up detail shots of the chronometer — far exceeding the 3 to 4 reference photos standard for insurance documentation. Six images were taken from beneath the base plate where the serial number and maker's mark are engraved: precisely the data required to commission a convincing custom replica.

Phone records show Odette received a call at 12:47 PM — sixteen minutes before entering the display room — from a marine antiques dealer in Bristol flagged by Lloyd's of London for facilitating two prior insurance-fraud claims involving period nautical instruments. A review of Odette's prior inspection files shows she personally photographed the chronometer during the previous year's valuation, four months before a specialty prop manufacturer's records show a custom order for a 'Kendall K2-style chronometer, 19th-century aesthetic.'

exonerations.nora: Nora locked the case at 9:15 AM and her badge shows no further access. The morning viewing was attended by eleven members who can confirm the piece was authentic at closing. The sensor log records no case opening between 9:15 AM and Odette's 1:03 PM entry.
exonerations.flynn: Flynn's lecture ran from 11 AM to 12:30 PM in the adjoining room with 22 attendees; none reported him leaving during the session. The sensor log records no access event attributable to Flynn's badge at any point during the day.
exonerations.graham: Graham's vessel departure was logged by the marina dock system at 9:52 AM and his return at 12:07 PM. His club bar tab, opened at 12:15 PM, places him in the bar for the hour before and after Odette's 1:03 PM entry.
exonerations.ivan: Ivan's work order placed him in the adjacent office the entire two-hour window. The office security camera confirms he was at the drawer throughout and never crossed the threshold into the display room.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes Odette's 44-minute session as the only case opening after 9:15 AM, and the chemically accelerated patina on the facsimile confirms the substitution was arranged well in advance — not improvised on site. Clue 2 shows what Odette was actually doing during those 44 minutes: 31 close-up photographs of the chronometer including six of the maker's mark and serial number beneath the base plate — exactly the data needed to commission a replica to specification. Clue 3 closes the loop: a Bristol dealer flagged for nautical instrument fraud called Odette sixteen minutes before she entered the room, and a replica order matching the chronometer's specifications was placed four months after her prior-year inspection gave her every technical detail she needed. Nora's 9:15 AM lock, Flynn's 22-person lecture, Graham's bar tab, and Ivan's office camera eliminate every other suspect. Odette had used her previous year's visit to document the piece for a replica commission, arranged collection through her Bristol contact, and spent her 44-minute 'inspection' making the swap.

title: The Overnight Manifest
case: A sealed crate containing $94,000 worth of uncut gemstones — consigned for delivery to a jeweler — was opened at Calverton Freight Depot and the stones replaced with mineral specimens of similar weight and appearance. The crate passed outbound inspection at 7 PM with its tamper band intact; the substitution was discovered by the receiving jeweler the following morning.

suspects.name: Dex
suspects.alibi: I came on at 7 PM after the outbound check. The crate was already sealed and palletized when my shift started.

suspects.name: Lin
suspects.alibi: I stacked the pallet at 4 PM with the tamper band on. My shift ended at 5 PM — I didn't go near it after that.

suspects.name: Marta
suspects.alibi: I picked up the pallet at 9 PM and drove straight to the delivery hub. I never opened anything in the cargo hold.

suspects.name: Stellan
suspects.alibi: I opened the crate at 2 PM, confirmed the stones against the manifest, resealed it with a fresh tamper band, and left. Standard verification procedure.

suspects.name: Bev
suspects.alibi: I was on gate duty from 6 AM to 6 PM and checked every outbound vehicle. Everything that left under my watch had complete paperwork.

starter: The crate's tamper band serial number was logged at two checkpoints: Stellan's 2 PM verification (band #TK-8814) and the outbound security check at 7 PM (band #TK-8814 confirmed intact). The receiving jeweler, however, found the crate sealed with band #TK-8815 — the immediately consecutive serial in the same production run. Depot procurement records show both numbers were issued to Stellan as part of a ten-band pack purchased through his firm's account six weeks prior.

The mineral specimens used as replacements — rough calcite clusters visually similar to uncut beryl — were identified as commercially available display minerals sold by a Dutch supply company. An order for two kilograms of 'rough calcite, green-tinted, irregular form' was placed through that company's online portal from an IP address registered to Stellan's firm, twelve days before his verification appointment at Calverton.

Cross-referencing freight depot records reveals a pattern across the preceding eighteen months: four high-value stone shipments had been verified by Stellan's firm, and three of those four were subsequently disputed by receiving parties for contents discrepancies. In each prior case, the tamper band at delivery differed by exactly one serial from the band logged at verification — a consistent signature of same-production-run substitution.

exonerations.dex: Dex came on shift at 7 PM, one hour after the outbound check confirmed band #TK-8814 intact. Security cameras show the palletized crate was loaded onto Marta's vehicle at 9:04 PM without Dex physically contacting it.
exonerations.lin: Lin sealed the pallet at 4 PM with band #TK-8814 intact, confirmed by the 7 PM outbound log. His shift ended at 5 PM; depot cameras show he did not return to the pallet area after clocking out.
exonerations.marta: GPS tracking on Marta's vehicle logs a direct route from depot to hub with no stops. The cargo compartment's door sensor recorded no open event at any point during the journey.
exonerations.bev: Bev's gate shift ended at 6:02 PM — confirmed by the clock-out log — a full hour before the 7 PM outbound check. She had no access to the depot floor after her shift ended.

reveal: Clue 1 exposes the mechanism: both band #TK-8814 and #TK-8815 were drawn from a pack issued exclusively to Stellan six weeks prior, meaning no one else had access to that consecutive sequence.
